past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 9 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with oral agents
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
family_history: Father died of lung cancer at age 71
Mother had Alzheimer's disease, died at age 84
Brother with hypertension and coronary artery disease
No family history of brain tumors or neurological conditions

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 18 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 30 minutes most days, plays golf weekly

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place, but intermittently confused about date. Speech is fluent but with occasional word-finding pauses. Able to follow two-step commands but makes errors on three-step commands. Attention mildly impaired on serial sevens (4 errors). Short-term recall 1 out of 3 objects at 5 minutes. MMSE approximately 24/30.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields reveal a left homonymous hemianopia on confrontation testing. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Mild left lower facial droop. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper and lower extremities 5/5 throughout. Left upper extremity: deltoid 4/5, biceps 4/5, triceps 4/5, grip 4-/5, finger extensors 4/5. Left lower extremity: hip flexion 4+/5, knee extension 5/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Mild left pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased light touch and pinprick in the left hand and forearm. Proprioception intact in bilateral great toes. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right upper extremity 2+, right lower extremity 2+. Left upper extremity 3+, left lower extremity 3+ with extensor plantar response on the left (positive Babinski sign). Right plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose slightly dysmetric on the left, intact on the right. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Mild left-sided circumduction gait. Able to walk independently but with subtle left foot drop. Tandem gait impaired. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Papilledema noted on fundoscopic examination bilaterally, more prominent on the right. No meningismus.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headache, left arm weakness, and a new-onset seizure
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Kenji Watanabe is a 71-year-old right-handed retired mathematics teacher who presents to the emergency department after experiencing a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home approximately two hours ago. His wife reports that over the past three weeks he has been complaining of progressively worsening right-sided headaches that are worst in the morning upon waking and occasionally awaken him from sleep. He initially attributed these to tension headaches and self-treated with over-the-counter ibuprofen with only partial relief. Over the past ten days, she has noticed increasing difficulty with his left hand — he has been dropping objects, had trouble buttoning his shirt, and his handwriting has become noticeably clumsier. She also reports subtle personality changes over the past two weeks, describing him as more irritable and less engaged in conversation than usual. Three days ago he began complaining of intermittent blurry vision, particularly in his left visual field, noting he bumped into a doorframe on his left side. This morning, while eating breakfast, his wife witnessed him suddenly stiffen, drop his fork, and fall from his chair onto the floor with rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately 90 seconds. He was briefly unresponsive afterward and gradually became confused and drowsy. EMS was called and upon their arrival approximately 10 minutes later, he was postictal with slurred speech and marked left-sided weakness. He was transported to the emergency department where he has gradually become more alert over the past hour. He has no prior history of seizures. He denies recent head trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or weight loss, although his wife notes his appetite has been reduced over the past two weeks and he may have lost a few pounds.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha, attenuated over the right hemisphere
background.symmetry: Asymmetric, with attenuation and slowing over the right frontoparietal region
background.reactivity: Present bilaterally but reduced over the right hemisphere

findings.state: Present in both wakefulness and drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with focal structural lesion in the right frontoparietal region

findings.morphology: Sharp contour with following slow wave, phase reversal at F4
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptiform discharges in peri-tumoral cortex, correlating with recent seizure

findings.morphology: Reduced amplitude and frequency over the right hemisphere compared to left
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with right hemispheric structural pathology with mass effect

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to suspected elevated intracranial pressure
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response; no change in focal findings

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating continuous right frontoparietal polymorphic delta activity with intermittent right frontal sharp waves and hemispheric background asymmetry. Findings are consistent with a right frontoparietal structural lesion with associated epileptogenic potential. The focal slowing and epileptiform discharges correlate with the patient's recent seizure and clinical presentation.
eeg.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to clinical concern for elevated intracranial pressure.
recommended_actions: Urgent brain MRI with and without contrast
Initiation of antiepileptic medication for seizure prophylaxis
Continuous EEG monitoring if further seizure activity is suspected

findings.type: Heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis
findings.location: Right frontoparietal lobe, centered at the junction of the precentral and superior frontal gyri, extending into the corona radiata and abutting the body of the right lateral ventricle
findings.size: 5.8 x 4.3 x 4.7 cm in maximal dimensions

signal_characteristics.T1_pre: Heterogeneous hypointensity with areas of central T1 shortening suggesting hemorrhagic necrosis
signal_characteristics.T1_post: Thick, irregular peripheral ring enhancement with enhancing nodular components along the medial and posterior margins
signal_characteristics.T2: Heterogeneous hyperintensity centrally (necrosis) with surrounding hyperintense edema
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Extensive perilesional hyperintensity extending throughout the right frontal and parietal white matter
signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion along the enhancing rim indicating high cellularity
signal_characteristics.ADC: Low ADC values at the enhancing rim; elevated ADC centrally consistent with necrosis
signal_characteristics.SWI: Foci of susceptibility within the lesion suggesting intratumoral hemorrhage

findings.mass_effect: Effacement of the right lateral ventricle body. Rightward subfalcine herniation with 8 mm leftward midline shift at the level of the septum pellucidum. Compression of the right cingulate gyrus.
findings.borders: Irregular, infiltrative margins that blend with surrounding white matter on FLAIR sequences

additional_observations: No additional enhancing lesions identified in the brain parenchyma
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss appropriate for age
Mucosal thickening in bilateral maxillary sinuses
mri.impression: 1. Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontoparietal mass (5.8 x 4.3 x 4.7 cm) with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, restricted diffusion at the enhancing rim, and surrounding vasogenic edema. 2. Significant mass effect with 8 mm leftward midline shift and effacement of the right lateral ventricle. 3. Imaging appearance is highly characteristic of a high-grade glioma (WHO grade IV glioblastoma). 4. No evidence of multifocal disease or leptomeningeal dissemination.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, thick irregular rim enhancement, restricted diffusion at rim, infiltrative borders, and single lesion in frontoparietal white matter
Solitary lesion without known primary malignancy; infiltrative borders favor glioma over metastasis which typically has well-defined margins at gray-white junction
Central necrosis and ring enhancement pattern less typical of lymphoma; lymphoma typically enhances homogeneously and is periventricular
No clinical signs of infection (afebrile, normal WBC); thick irregular wall favors tumor over abscess (abscess typically has thin smooth rim)

recommended_actions: Neurosurgical consultation for biopsy or resection planning
MR perfusion imaging to confirm elevated rCBV in enhancing components
Initiate dexamethasone for vasogenic edema and mass effect
Pre-surgical functional MRI if lesion is near eloquent cortex

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes and post-seizure stress response

Other.clinical_significance: Indicates diabetes, reasonably controlled on current regimen
Mildly elevated, may be related to tumor-associated inflammation or post-seizure state

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are largely within normal limits. CBC, BMP (except glucose), coagulation studies, and hepatic panel are normal, establishing an adequate pre-surgical baseline. Glucose is mildly elevated consistent with known diabetes and post-seizure stress. HbA1c of 6.8% indicates reasonably controlled diabetes. CRP is mildly elevated, which may be tumor-related or post-seizure. LDH is within normal range. Normal renal and hepatic function supports candidacy for chemotherapy if needed.
abnormal_values_summary: Glucose: 151 mg/dL - Mildly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes and post-seizure stress response
HbA1c: 7.3 % - Indicates diabetes, reasonably controlled on current regimen
CRP: 4.2 mg/L - Mildly elevated, may be related to tumor-associated inflammation or post-seizure state

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right frontoparietal mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Markedly elevated rCBV ratio of 4.8 (relative to contralateral normal white matter) in the enhancing rim
signal_characteristics.rCBF: Elevated rCBF in the enhancing tumor components
signal_characteristics.Ktrans: Elevated permeability consistent with disrupted blood-brain barrier

findings.location: Central necrotic core of the mass

signal_characteristics.rCBV: Markedly reduced rCBV centrally, consistent with necrosis

additional_observations: rCBV values in the enhancing rim exceed the threshold of 1.75, strongly suggesting high-grade neoplasm
No areas of elevated perfusion outside the enhancing lesion to suggest infiltrative non-enhancing tumor
output.impression: MR perfusion demonstrates markedly elevated rCBV (ratio 4.8) within the enhancing rim of the right frontoparietal mass, highly consistent with high-grade glioma (WHO grade IV). The elevated rCBV significantly exceeds the threshold of 1.75 used to distinguish high-grade from low-grade gliomas. Central necrotic region shows expected absence of perfusion.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: rCBV ratio of 4.8 is characteristic of glioblastoma; elevated permeability confirms neovascularization
Low-grade gliomas typically have rCBV ratios below 1.75; the markedly elevated rCBV essentially excludes this

recommended_actions: Findings support urgent neurosurgical planning for maximal safe resection
MR spectroscopy for additional metabolic characterization
Tissue sampling for molecular markers (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q)

findings.location: Enhancing rim of the right frontoparietal mass

signal_characteristics.Lipid_Lactate: Prominent lipid and lactate peaks, consistent with necrosis and anaerobic metabolism
Dominant lipid peak consistent with necrosis

additional_observations: Cho/NAA ratio of 5.2 at the enhancing rim is highly characteristic of high-grade malignancy
Peritumoral spectroscopy shows mild choline elevation, suggesting possible infiltrative tumor beyond the enhancing margin
Contralateral hemisphere shows normal metabolite ratios
output.impression: MR spectroscopy demonstrates markedly elevated choline with reduced NAA and prominent lipid/lactate peaks in the enhancing rim, a metabolite profile highly consistent with high-grade glioma. The Cho/NAA ratio of 5.2 strongly favors a WHO grade IV neoplasm. Lipid/lactate peaks suggest necrosis and anaerobic metabolism. Mild choline elevation in peritumoral tissue suggests infiltrative disease beyond the enhancing margin.

differential_by_imaging.key_features: Markedly elevated Cho/NAA ratio, prominent lipid/lactate, and reduced NAA are characteristic
Abscess would show amino acid peaks (valine, leucine, isoleucine) and succinate; these are absent

recommended_actions: Tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling remains the gold standard
Spectroscopy data supports high-grade diagnosis and can guide biopsy targeting to most metabolically active region

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: No pulmonary masses or nodules identified. Mild bibasilar dependent atelectasis.
Homogeneous hepatic parenchyma without focal lesions. No hepatomegaly.
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement. Simple 1.2 cm left renal cyst. No suspicious masses.
Normal in size and attenuation. No masses.
Mildly enlarged prostate gland. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Urinary bladder normal.
CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.clinical_significance: Enlarged prostate consistent with known BPH

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. No suspicious osseous lesions.

output.interpretation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis reveals no evidence of a primary malignancy or metastatic disease. No pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, or lymphadenopathy identified. Incidental findings include a simple left renal cyst and enlarged prostate consistent with known BPH. This effectively rules out the brain lesion being a metastasis from a systemic primary cancer, supporting the diagnosis of a primary brain tumor.

Histopathology.clinical_significance: Confirms high-grade malignant glioma, the most aggressive primary brain tumor

Molecular Markers.clinical_significance: IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with poorer prognosis compared to IDH-mutant
MGMT methylation predicts better response to temozolomide chemotherapy; favorable prognostic marker
Absence of 1p/19q codeletion is expected in glioblastoma; codeletion is characteristic of oligodendroglioma
Common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma, supports the diagnosis
Common molecular alteration in glioblastoma; potential therapeutic target
High proliferation index consistent with aggressive tumor biology

output.interpretation: Stereotactic biopsy confirms glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV, with classic histological features including pseudopalisading necrosis, microvascular proliferation, high cellularity, and elevated mitotic activity. Molecular profiling reveals IDH-wildtype status (poorer prognosis), MGMT promoter methylation (favorable for temozolomide response), TERT promoter mutation, and EGFR amplification. The absence of 1p/19q codeletion is consistent with glioblastoma rather than oligodendroglioma. Ki-67 proliferation index of 35% confirms aggressive tumor biology. These findings support standard treatment with maximal safe surgical resection followed by concurrent chemoradiation with temozolomide (Stupp protocol).
abnormal_values_summary: Histological Diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV  - Confirms high-grade malignant glioma, the most aggressive primary brain tumor
Cellularity: High, with marked nuclear pleomorphism  - High-grade features
Mitotic Activity: Elevated, >10 mitoses per 10 high-power fields  - Indicates rapid cell proliferation
Microvascular Proliferation: Present, with glomeruloid vascular proliferation  - Hallmark feature of glioblastoma
Necrosis: Pseudopalisading necrosis present  - Pathognomonic feature of glioblastoma
IDH1/IDH2 Mutation: Wildtype (not mutated)  - IDH-wildtype glioblastoma; associated with poorer prognosis compared to IDH-mutant
MGMT Promoter Methylation: Methylated  - MGMT methylation predicts better response to temozolomide chemotherapy; favorable prognostic marker
TERT Promoter Mutation: Present  - Common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma, supports the diagnosis
EGFR Amplification: Present  - Common molecular alteration in glioblastoma; potential therapeutic target
Ki-67 Proliferation Index: 35%  - High proliferation index consistent with aggressive tumor biology

signal_characteristics.activation_pattern: Right primary motor cortex activation for left hand tasks is identified along the posterior margin of the tumor, with activation foci displaced posteriorly and medially by the mass
signal_characteristics.distance_to_tumor: Approximately 6 mm from the enhancing tumor margin to the nearest motor activation cluster
signal_characteristics.laterality_index: Right-hemisphere dominant motor activation for left hand as expected

findings.mass_effect: Tumor displaces the motor strip posteriorly

signal_characteristics.activation_pattern: Broca's area (left inferior frontal gyrus) and Wernicke's area (left superior temporal gyrus) show robust activation during language tasks
signal_characteristics.distance_to_tumor: Contralateral to tumor; no language areas at risk from surgical approach

additional_observations: Supplementary motor area (SMA) on the right shows activation medial and superior to the tumor; may be at risk during resection of superior tumor margin
DTI tractography shows displacement of the right corticospinal tract posteriorly and medially by the mass but the tract remains intact
output.impression: Functional MRI demonstrates that the right primary motor cortex (hand area) is displaced posteriorly by the tumor and lies approximately 6 mm from the enhancing margin. The supplementary motor area is also in proximity to the superior tumor extent. Language areas are in the contralateral left hemisphere and are not at risk. DTI tractography confirms displacement but preservation of the right corticospinal tract. Intraoperative mapping with cortical stimulation is recommended to maximize safe resection while preserving motor function.
recommended_actions: Intraoperative cortical and subcortical stimulation mapping for motor preservation
Awake craniotomy may be considered but is not strictly necessary given language lateralization to the contralateral hemisphere
Neuronavigation integration of fMRI and DTI data for surgical planning
Postoperative MRI within 48 hours to assess extent of resection

interactions: Dexamethasone + Metformin: Corticosteroids can cause hyperglycemia, potentially reducing the effectiveness of metformin. Blood glucose monitoring is essential, and insulin may be required for glycemic control during steroid administration.
Dexamethasone + Aspirin: Concurrent use increases the risk of gastrointestinal bleeding. Consider gastroprotective therapy with a proton pump inhibitor.
warnings: Monitor blood glucose closely given the patient's diabetes; corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia is common and may require sliding-scale insulin
Long-term use associated with immunosuppression, osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, and myopathy
Taper gradually when discontinuing to avoid adrenal crisis
alternatives: Bevacizumab (for refractory cerebral edema not responding to steroids)

warnings: May cause behavioral side effects including irritability, agitation, and mood changes; monitor given the patient's recent personality changes
Dose adjustment may be needed for renal impairment (patient's creatinine is normal)
Well-tolerated antiepileptic with minimal hepatic enzyme interactions, making it preferred in patients who will receive chemotherapy
alternatives: Valproic acid (alternative, but with more drug interactions and hepatotoxicity risk)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade IV, right frontoparietal lobe

differential.key_distinguishing: Solitary lesion with infiltrative borders favors primary glioma; no primary malignancy identified on CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; metastases typically have well-defined borders at gray-white junction
Primary CNS lymphoma typically enhances homogeneously and is periventricular; central necrosis and thick irregular rim enhancement are more characteristic of glioblastoma; patient is immunocompetent
Patient is afebrile with normal WBC; abscess has thin smooth enhancing rim and restricted diffusion centrally (pus); no source of infection identified
Typically presents in younger patients with open ring enhancement and less mass effect relative to size; no history of multiple sclerosis; marked mass effect with thick rim enhancement favors GBM

optimal_actions.action: Perform initial stabilization: secure airway, obtain IV access, assess vital signs, and perform focused neurological exam including GCS
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Post-ictal patient with focal neurological deficits (left-sided weakness, hemianopia)

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to evaluate for mass lesion, hemorrhage, or midline shift
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large right frontal mass with surrounding edema and midline shift

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, hepatic panel, and LDH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal baseline labs suitable for pre-surgical workup; mild hyperglycemia

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus then 4 mg IV every 6 hours for vasogenic edema and elevated intracranial pressure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in peritumoral edema and improvement in neurological symptoms over 24-48 hours

optimal_actions.action: Initiate levetiracetam 500 mg IV twice daily for seizure prophylaxis
Obtain brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast to characterize the mass
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Large heterogeneously enhancing right frontoparietal mass with central necrosis, ring enhancement, and significant mass effect consistent with high-grade glioma

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to characterize seizure focus and assess for subclinical seizure activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Focal slowing over right frontoparietal region with epileptiform discharges in peri-tumoral cortex

optimal_actions.action: Consult neurosurgery for surgical planning including biopsy or maximal safe resection
Order CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis to rule out primary malignancy (metastasis workup)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No evidence of systemic malignancy, supporting primary brain tumor diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain MR perfusion to confirm elevated rCBV in enhancing components
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated rCBV ratio in enhancing rim, consistent with high-grade neoplasm

optimal_actions.action: Obtain functional MRI for pre-surgical motor and language mapping given proximity to motor cortex
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Motor cortex displaced but identifiable near tumor margin; language lateralized to contralateral hemisphere

optimal_actions.action: Proceed with maximal safe surgical resection with intraoperative mapping, followed by Stupp protocol (concurrent temozolomide and radiation therapy, then adjuvant temozolomide)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Tissue diagnosis confirming glioblastoma with molecular profiling guiding adjuvant therapy

critical_actions: Obtain emergent neuroimaging (CT or MRI) to identify the mass lesion causing seizure and focal deficits
Initiate dexamethasone to reduce vasogenic edema and intracranial pressure
Initiate antiepileptic medication for seizure control and prophylaxis
Obtain MRI with contrast for definitive characterization of the mass
Consult neurosurgery for tissue diagnosis and surgical planning
Perform systemic workup (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) to rule out metastatic disease
Obtain tissue diagnosis with molecular profiling (IDH, MGMT, 1p/19q) to guide treatment
contraindicated_actions: Performing lumbar puncture in the setting of a large mass with midline shift (risk of uncal herniation)
Administering phenytoin or carbamazepine as first-line antiepileptic (enzyme inducers that interact with chemotherapy agents)
Delaying dexamethasone in a patient with significant mass effect and papilledema
Starting empiric temozolomide without tissue diagnosis and molecular profiling
Performing whole-brain radiation without first establishing a tissue diagnosis
Administering contrast dye without checking renal function in a diabetic patient
key_reasoning_points: New-onset seizure in a 61-year-old with progressive headache and focal neurological deficits warrants urgent neuroimaging to evaluate for a structural lesion
Morning headaches worse upon waking, progressive over three weeks with papilledema, indicate elevated intracranial pressure from an expanding mass
MRI findings of a large heterogeneously enhancing mass with central necrosis, thick irregular ring enhancement, and restricted diffusion at the rim are classic for glioblastoma
EEG showing focal polymorphic delta activity and epileptiform discharges over the right frontoparietal region correlates with the MRI findings and localizes the seizure focus
Metastasis workup with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is essential to differentiate a primary brain tumor from a solitary metastasis, even when imaging is suggestive of glioma

Dexamethasone should be initiated promptly for vasogenic edema with significant mass effect, but clinicians should be aware of its effects on blood glucose in diabetic patients and its potential to alter biopsy histology in lymphoma
Levetiracetam is preferred over enzyme-inducing antiepileptics (phenytoin, carbamazepine) in brain tumor patients because it does not interfere with chemotherapy metabolism
MGMT promoter methylation status is the most important molecular marker for predicting temozolomide response and guiding treatment decisions in glioblastoma

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic presentation with progressive headache, left arm weakness, new-onset seizure, and a large right frontoparietal mass with midline shift on imaging
clinical_pearls: Glioblastoma is the most common and most aggressive primary brain tumor in adults, with median survival of 14-16 months with standard treatment
The Stupp protocol (surgical resection followed by 6 weeks concurrent temozolomide/radiation then 6 cycles adjuvant temozolomide) remains the standard of care
MGMT promoter methylation is present in approximately 40-45% of glioblastomas and is associated with improved survival with temozolomide therapy
New-onset seizure in an adult over 40 should always raise suspicion for a brain tumor until proven otherwise
